answer:
**Manu’s *Manusmṛti* (Book 1) frames the present as the declining Kali Yuga within a vast cosmic cycle.** The text outlines four successive *yugas* (ages)—Kṛta (4,000 divine years, plus equal twilights before/after), Tretā, Dvāpara, and Kali (1,000 divine years)—forming a single *mahāyuga* of 12,000 divine years (4,320,000 human years).[^1] These repeat endlessly within *manvantaras* (71 *mahāyugas*, ruled by successive Manus) and Brahma’s day/night (a thousand *mahāyugas* each), with creation/dissolution at each scale.[^2] Dharma (righteousness) loses one “foot” per age; lifespans drop from 400 years (Kṛta) to 100 (Kali); austerity yields to charity as the supreme virtue.[^3] The present is explicitly Kali, with diminished human power, as duties and lifespans “bear fruit... according to the age.”[^4]

**Cynewulf’s poems (*Andreas*, *Elene*, *Juliana*) frame the present as mid-arc in a singular, irreversible history from creation to final judgment.** All invoke God as “eternal... of all creation,” ruling a linear cosmos from Genesis (“first works”) through Christ’s life/death/resurrection to Doom.[^5] No cyclic decline or renewal; history progresses toward eschaton: the world “shall pass away” in flame at Christ’s return with angels, souls judged by deeds into eternal states—heaven for righteous (refined like gold), hell for wicked, purgatorial fire for middling.[^6] Saints’ lives (*Andreas*’ mission, *Elene*’s cross-finding, *Juliana*’s martyrdom) exemplify this arc: post-Incarnation era of evangelism amid persecution, hastening Doom. Cynewulf’s runic signature pleads mercy at judgment, underscoring personal stakes in linear accountability.[^7]

**Cynewulf lacks any yuga-analogue; his salvation history is irreducibly linear.** No descending/repeating eras (searches for “six ages,” “world ages,” *yuga*-terms yield none). “Ages past” are historical (“days long departed”), not cosmic phases.[^8] Eschatology is teleological: creation → fall → Incarnation → church age → judgment → eternity (no pralaya/renewal). *Elene*’s fire-judgment evokes refinement, not Manu’s sleep/awake cycles.[^9] Present toil builds eternal reward, contrasting Manu’s inevitable decay until dissolution.

[^1]: *Manusmṛti*, Ch. 1, [¶68-71](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/01/translation/#p-a22081).
[^2]: *Manusmṛti*, Ch. 1, [¶71-80](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/01/translation/#p-03b23e).
[^3]: *Manusmṛti*, Ch. 1, [¶81-86](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/01/translation/#p-fcafde).
[^4]: *Manusmṛti*, Ch. 1, [¶83-85](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/01/translation/#p-abd27d).
[^5]: *Andreas*, Ch. 1, [ll. 355-70](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-355089); *Elene*, Ch. 1, [ll. 835ff.](/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/translation/#p-f5d1f1).
[^6]: *Elene*, Ch. 1, [ll. 1228-79](/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/translation/#p-dcbbfe).
[^7]: *Juliana*, Ch. 1, [ll. 715ff.](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-e769dc).
[^8]: *Elene*, Ch. 1, [l. 1](/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/translation/#p-222dfa); corpus searches confirm no cycles.
[^9]: *Elene*, Ch. 1, [ll. 1245-60](/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/translation/#p-dcbbfe).
